There's two types of people. There's
illuminators and there's diminishers.
>> And illuminators shine the light on the
individual. They're curious. They're
open. They're getting to know the
person. It's about them. Diminishers
make it about them.
>> They tie it back to their own stories.
They're toppers. They hear a story and
then they'll top with their own story.
When I was a rep. And the reality is
reps rarely care what their sales
manager did as a [music] performer.
Really good sales leaders forget about
what they did as individual contributors
and are solely focused on illuminating
the talents and helping the other people
become the best they can be.

>> Yeah, it's a great question.
Results.
>> Tell me more. And I I think when you

account size.

those numbers and did they get the
result or was it sourced by marketing?
Was it led with a with a team of
national account people to help get that
number?
>> So then you back into, you know, based
off of that result, are they taking sole
credit for something that was really a
team effort? And I think acknowledging a
team effort is really important too in
sales. So in a team environment, you you
do want a sales rep that utilizes
resources. So if they're saying this was
the result, they're not able to back
into it. They can't justify how they got
to that number. They don't truly
understand the math behind it or they
take credit for things that were really
a team approach. I think those are some
warning signs early on. I think it's
really interesting is um I think when
you get to that level of granularity, I
think a lot of leaders are scared to ask
those questions. And I think a lot of
times what I've seen as well is those
reps, they also don't know how they got
to those numbers

performers versus low performers.
>> And and I think the reality is is that
companies have built systems, processes,
and policies for their salespeople
>> based off the lowest third performers or

>> Uh compensation plans a lot of times are

base salary and commission function. I I
I find it interesting when salespeople

you're hiring them versus negotiate
commissions.
>> So if you can negotiate a base salary or
in security or negotiate, hey, I'm going
to I'm betting on myself.
>> Now I sold I sold doortodoor 100%
commission. I was a 1099 all through
college. Did it for four plus years.
>> And there's no salary. you get what you
are what the value that you're bringing
every single day.
And so when I got my first corporate
job, I looked at the base salary, which
I really didn't care about. I just said,
"Show me, can I see the commission
plan?"
>> And I remember asking a little naive,
but I said, "Is there a way I can just
do full commission?"
>> Because I'm looking at the commission
plan and I'm thinking, "Okay, I can I
have confidence. I know I can kill it."
But I I see that they're supplementing
my ability and my strength and my
capacity to sell. They're they're taking
away from that and and putting a base
salary on it. Now, all companies have a
base salary. But I I think if you're
attracting true achievers and
resultsoriented people that are
competitive and they want to be their
their absolute best,
>> then they want to look at that
commission plan or the bonus structure
and say, "How can I achieve more from
that?" And if we're stuck on the base
salary and they don't know the
commission plan or haven't asked to see
the commission plan, then that would be
a yellow flag for me.
>> That's really interesting. What's what's
actually really funny is as you were
talking, I remember this is well over a
decade ago. You actually told me
something very you told me that same
story. I'm like I'm like that is such an
interesting shift, right? And and I
think it makes a lot of sense,
especially if you're selling in a more

deal cycle they're they're going to
close less deals but it might take six
to 12 months to even close one deal.

think that can still attract the right
people at the same time those people

things in their life. How would you

>> Yeah. Well, I think a base salary always

>> Um, but I think if there's a way to

>> I think you want to lean into that as
much as you can.
So, you know, do some companies look at
commissions as an expense or as a as a
cost on the P&L commission percent. And
so they're driving, you know, how can we
pay less commissions as a percent to
revenue. And so what happens is you get
stuck into believing that revenue costs
something
>> and revenue and and but commission rates
gain revenue. The truth is is that the
more commission you're paying, the more
revenue that you're making. So to
squeeze profitability, oftentimes we
look at lowering commission rates. And I
think it has the opposite effect. And
I've seen that.
>> Oh, yeah.
>> Things are tight. We're going to lower
commission rates. Well, if we can
incentivize more profitable business or
pay commission rates on profit, if your
percent to to sales revenue right now is
15%. And you're thinking, how do we get
it down to 12? Well, you're going to
lose revenue. And I would submit that
you're probably going to lose more
revenue than you're going to gain in
cost savings.
>> Oh yeah, 100%.
>> So we fall into this trap sometimes in
trying to cut costs, but I think
commission dollars is one of the worst
places you can go to cut cost. Lowering
commission rates or making it more
difficult to make the commissions is
going to cost a lot of revenue. Yeah, I
think this is where
this is why it is such a mistake for so
many leaders to manage via just pure
numbers and like a Excel spreadsheet or
a Google sheet, right? Because if you
look at the dollar dollar-wise, if
you're like, oh, you know, like the

sales is is going up because of
commissions, right? But what's the cost
when we decrease it? What's the actual
true cost of the growth of the
organization as a result? Like what
happens is like you have a lower
commission, right? What happens? You get
you hire worse people.
>> Yeah.
>> Right. You hire people who are okay with
not making that much money. People who

Well, I think it's really interesting

right, I think the mistake that you

many leaders basically try to hire
essentially many clones of themselves,
right? So you just and suddenly you see
this whole sales or it's basically same
version of the same person, right? And
it can be good in some ways because you
all have certain strengths. But I think
about even myself if I just hire a bunch
of people just like me, we all the same
weaknesses too. So all my flaws now are
magnified. And I get a bunch of yes
people in the organization and and I had
learned this the hard way, right? And
and I remember looking back when and my
big shift was really seeing teams like a
mosaic.

>> or the opportunity for success. So, you
were a very lowrisk, high opportunity

risk um but also a really high upside.
This could go either way. I'm fully

>> I'm beating my number.
>> I want to
>> I want to I want to risk a little bit
here." And that's okay as long as you
know.
>> The worst time to interview and hire
somebody, Marcus, is when you need
somebody.
>> Yep. That's the absolute worst time to
interview is that when you're down
somebody. I always did interviews
throughout, even if I was fully staffed.
I would try to have five uh or so
interviews every single week,
>> even when I was fully staffed.
>> Oh, yeah.
>> And I never became underststaffed
because I would always fight to be
overstaffed or at least have people on
the bench that I could call. So,
building relationships and networks.
>> But I I do think the worst time to
interview is when you need somebody. And
so when you talk about how many people
that I've interviewed, which which is
over 5,000, it's because, you know, five
or 10 a week over the course of 25
years, it adds up. And it seems like a
big number, but when you're when you're
in the grind of just doing the thing,
right, just interviewing constantly
>> and looking for talent,
>> I think that's one of the greatest
things that I ever learned and did as a
sales leader is I was always looking
whether or not I was staffed for talent.
sales reps that called on our company. I
recruited many people that tried to sell
me something.
>> Um, I recruited a door-to-d dooror rep
time selling vacuums. Sold me a curvy.
>> Still have the vacuum
>> 15 years later.
>> So, always recruiting, always looking
for talent. And I and if we do if a
sales leader operates that way that I'm
always looking for A players and I'm
always going to interview for A players
regardless of staffing levels then the
opportunity that they're going to find
the A players and succeed just increases
exponentially.
>> So I think a couple key things I think I
love how you broke that down because I
think number one you're 100% right when
you are underst staffed is the worst
time to hire because you get desperate.
It's kind of like a rep who get who gets
commission breath because they're
they're behind their number. It's the
same thing. You you you close deals you
probably shouldn't close. You bring
people you probably shouldn't bring in.
You know, risk tolerance is like much
higher in which obviously, you know,
there's a long tale of negative effects
if it's the wrong type of hire. Um and I
love the the positioning of always
always be recruiting, right? I've always
been a firm believers
to be a collector of talent. Like
wherever I'm at, I'm want to be a
collector of talent. Maybe it's not
right now, but maybe in the future
there's a way we can work together in
some way, shape, or form, right? And
when I think about this, I think a lot
of leaders, they're not thinking this
way. So many execs I talk to and even
frontline managers, directors, VPs,
they're not proactive in recruiting and
finding people. Like what's maybe an
easy way to ease into it? I think a lot
of them, they get overwhelmed. They're
like, "Well, I'm already busy. I'm
already stressed already, you know, like
what's the easy way to ease into that
recruiting habit?
>> Yeah, this is a framework for me. It's
the way they're thinking about the
problem. And I hear this a lot and I'm
told this a lot about, "Hey, I know
you're busy, but so in my mind,
>> hamsters run about seven miles a night.
They run a marathon every four or five
days." Okay,
>> it's amazing.
>> Look it up.
>> That's a real stat.
>> It's a real stat. They're busy.
>> They're busy all night. Yeah. They're
busy all the time.
>> And they are in the cage. They step into
their circle. They run seven miles. In a
few days, they'll run a marathon. And
they get out in their exact in the exact
same spot.
>> So, I don't take pride in being busy.
>> I take pride in prioritizing
uh looking at what needs to be done.
>> And and so, if people say, "Travis, hey,
I I know you're busy."
every once in a while just have a
conversation about the difference
between filling my day and being busy
versus priority and and and
productivity.
>> Yeah.
>> So, the way that we frame up because
when you said that, that's the story
they're tell. I'm too busy to interview
all the time
>> and so then we're just chasing our own
tail because we're not fixing the
problem.
>> I'm too busy to go to the doctor and get

you know, pain medication. That's
>> right. I'm too busy to uh,

new one. So,
>> I think if you look at the framework on
how you view productivity and busy, I
think it can shift.
>> Yeah.
>> Because look look at the objective that
we're going for overall. What what are
we what's the objective? The objective
is to have a high performing team of A
players.
>> If that's the objective, then where's
the priority? Well, the priority is
finding A players to come into the team.
So, you can spend all the time you want
doing all the other activities, but I
think that's a misunderstanding of how
results are achieved.
>> So, what are you willing to give up in
order to get the a talent? What time are
you willing to sacrifice? What
activities are you willing to give up
even the ones you might like in order
for something greater, which is
attracting talent? Well, I think it's
it's such a good point and I think so
many leaders at all levels, they run
around chasing their tail or they're
that hamster on the hamster wheel
running seven miles per night, right?
And they get done with their day or week
or month or quarter. Like, what
happened? Suddenly, they're
understaffed. They're missing their
number. They're like, "Oh my god,
pipeline looks terrible. Future looks
terrible. What are we doing?" And I and
you you said right here, I want to
reemphasize it, but really what it
breaks down to a great sales leader does
really two things only. like they
basically find amazing A+ talent and
then they make them awesome and make
sure they stay save the company. That's
it.
>> Yeah.
>> Everything else is like just noise to
the role. And I remember when I stepped
into a director role and suddenly I had
uh nine sales managers and I realized
most of them didn't really internalize
that messaging and most of them weren't
even taking the time to go and like
recruit and bring people in. But I also
found the big key issue was they didn't
know how. They had no idea how. So I
literally had to like sit them down and
show them how to use LinkedIn jobs or
what whatever recruiter how to build
lists, how to do outreach, just like I
was teaching a rep how to do outreach.
And then we had even dedicated blocks
for outreach on their calendars, you
know, because it's like they needed that
time to prospect for great talent
because you and I both know like the
best way to always hit your number is to
be like you're over staffed with A+
talent and you have two, three, four,
five on the bench in case somebody gets
promoted, somebody decides that sales is
not for them, someone decides to move to
a different country and and become a
digital nomad, do something different,
right? Like you just never know because
you're managing human capital, right?
But if you are a collector of talent all
times, it's less stressful if you know
who the players are gonna be on your
team.
>> Yeah. The way I think about it is
exactly what you said. It's number one,
the job is to attract talent.
>> I think that's the number one job is to
recruit really good people.
>> If you look at all the really good
coaches, they're masters at recruiting.
They have the best recruiting classes.
So that's one of the first things is to
make sure you're always attracting good
talent. And then like you said, the
second one is to develop and retain the
talent.
>> Yeah. So if that's the the the functions
hire, develop, and retain, then our job
becomes actually really simple. And so
we can busy and have all the noise. We
can busy all that we want and have all
the noise that we want in terms of what
we're what we're focusing on or what
we're doing every day. But is the week
when you look back at the week or the
month, how much time did I spend in
attracting talent? And again, it's when
you're fully staffed, too.
>> Because when somebody leaves, they get
married, they move away, their mom gets
sick. We've all heard the stories. We
didn't We get that phone call.
>> Oh, yeah.
>> You know, something happened that you
couldn't control as a sales leader or
they got a different job, a better
opportunity, which we're all so happy
about for them,
>> right?
>> Always have an upgrade.
>> True.
>> Always have an upgrade from that talent.
>> Hire upgrade. So, the only way to do
that is not to throw the dice at it and
say, "Well, I'm down somebody, so now
I'll interview five people and see." No,
I mean, interview five a week for three
months. Mhm.
>> Right. Interview 60 people and then when
that time comes you'll be able to pick
the best person and you know recruiters
a lot of times internal or external
recruiters will will give three or four
candidates and I always said I want six
candidates at least eight ideally 10 and
I want to interview all of them and it's
a competing objective is for recruiters
or internal recruiters external
recruiters because their job is to place
people. Our job is to hire talent.
>> It's the war of competing. It's it's the
land of competing objectives.
>> So
>> when we focus on it proactively and we
sustain that, then we don't fall into
that trap.
>> Well, I think what's really interesting
as well is like, you know, if you if you
do that practice of doing five
interviews a week, what's amazing is the
level because that number of repetitions
you're doing, your radar for identifying
the right people goes up drastically.
Your filter becomes tighter and tighter,
right? like like I remember, you know,
like literally doing eight to 10
interviews a week. I was literally like
a sales job. So I felt like I was
interviewing eight interviews a week,
right? To make sure I could find the
best people, but also I was also making
sure it improve my skills as as an
interviewer. I can start to see through
the BS a little more. Start to get real
deeper into the numbers, start to
realize I'm like, "Oh man." And every
time I someone that fails a hire, that
was a feedback loop. What could I have
done early on the process to figure out
identify that they weren't a good fit
for our team? Exactly. They might still
be a great person. That's great, but
maybe not for my team.
>> Yeah.
>> Now, on the flip side, we talk about
retention, right? I think and that's a
big big piece I think a lot of leaders
don't care much about. Oh, we know how
expensive it is to replace people,
right? What are some good best practices
you've seen over time to really retain
your best talent?
>> Yeah. So this is this kind of goes into
my coaching leadership philosophy
>> which is as a coach our job is really
really simple coaching leadership is
help somebody get from where they are
today to where they want to go.
>> So the first thing that we want to know
as a sales leader that I want to know in
retaining talent is where where are you
going where you're going with your
career where do you want to end up
>> and if I can help them and support them
in that development then we're going to
we're going to ride together. It's when
the objectives miss. It's when they're
going a different direction than we're
going. That's when the disconnect and
that's where the turnover happens.
Turnover happens because there's a gap
between what you're offering as a leader
>> and what the company's offering and what
they desire.
>> That's right.
>> And that's that's the that's the and and
the greater the distance. So, I want to
be aligned where they're going.
>> Now, as long as it aligns with the
company's directives, then we're good.
if they're going a completely different
avenue. And I remember I was talking to
a rep one time and I said I asked a
question, where are you going? And and
what do you mean by that? I said, well,
sort of how however you want to take it.
>> And he said that his dad was an an
engineer and at the end of the day, he
wanted to work for his dad's company
because his dad was going to give him
the, you know, it was going to be handed
to him at some point. So he was out
cutting his teeth because a lot of times
that you go cut your teeth.
>> Um owners or founders of company if it's
going to pass on to generation they'll
say go outside of the company get more
knowledge cut your teeth then you got to
earn your way in. And so that's that's
what he was doing and he was able to
share that. So then it become everything
that we're talking about is to help get
results and learn the things that you
need to learn so that you'll be able to
>> take that next step. But while you're
here, while you're here, we're going to
help develop and help you become
>> Mhm.
>> you know, the best sales rep that you
can become. He's going to end up owning
his father's company. And that's going
to happen whether I want to retain him
or not. That's
>> right.
>> So, I believe that we can get the
discretionary effort, the effort that an
employee has to give, if they choose to
give it, if we can align where we're
going with where they want to go as a
coach and as a leader. So, I try to
flush that out early because they we can
retain the person longer. And the truth
is, Marcus is that you're never going to
well, it's very rare that you hire
somebody you you work with them for 30
years and they they work for you for 35
years until they retire. So if we don't
if we know the endgame is going to come
>> then it's how can we maximize our time
together so [clears throat] that we both
uh grow we're enriched we develop or the
best salesperson best leader that we can
become
>> during the time we have together and
when uh when I was having debriefs with
him every once in a while it was hey
how's it going with your with your
father's business hey no business is
growing
>> um and then it was well what do you
think you need to learn next in order to
take, you know, run the business that
day. And he's like, "Well, I'm not
sure." I said, "Why don't you go ask
your dad, hey, what what's a skill or
tool that I need to learn in order to
take over the business?" And once he
came back with that, then I said,
"Great. Let's focus on that in our
selling so that you can develop while
you're here into that." We had a great
relationship. Uh four or five years he
worked and then we knew the time was
coming and ultimately when he came into
my office and said, you know, it's time
to move on. It was gratitude. It was,
you know, we did what we needed to do.
Congratulations. We we're still in
contact. Still love to see the success.
And so developing people that do become
CEOs or owner owners of their own
business or helping develop those
people, there's a lot of satisfaction in
that. And I think if your brand as a
sales leader is that you are a developer
of people, then people in the market,
the longer you're a sales leader will
know that and they'll be attracted to
that and they'll want to work for you.
>> So you can recruit by becoming the best
leader in the moment with the people
because it's contagious and and word
spread. Sales people know sales people
and they talk.
>> Well, I think I think what's powerful is
it's a great philosophy in the way of
living life. I mean, you feel better as
a leader, right? versus treating people
like they're just a number to deliver a
number for you, right? And there's a
really good book called um carrots and
sticks don't work. I'm not sure if you
read it or not. They have something
called the respect model for leadership,
which is it stands for different word.
It's it's an acronym. So it's like
recognition, uh empowerment, um
supportive, P is partnering, E is
expectation, C is consideration, T is
trust. But the P partnering is what
we're talking about here. So you are
partnering with them to achieve their
own personal goals, right? And I think a
lot the mistake a lot of leaders make is
rep comes in or new employee comes in
for any any level. They say here's your
goal.
>> Here's your number. I want you to hit
this, you know, sell this much money or

presence. I want you to do these things.
>> Yeah.
>> Um
>> it's a trap.
>> It's a trap. So from a uh but what we're

like
>> hundreds of reps or dozens of reps or

these things.
>> Yeah.
>> Well I think keeping a record. Um, you
know, I always, um, when I was a sales

write it down.
>> Um, you know, even in the contact card.
Yeah.
>> In the notes section on my phone,
>> love it.
>> Kids names.
>> Uh, what's what are they going? What

>> And so as your team grows, you know, I

>> Hey, how did the game go last Friday?

yourself what the spouse's name, what
the kids kids name were, what the
>> So I think it's really important to
connect in a genuine way.
>> Oh yeah.
>> Um and there's an unsalesreated book
that I think relates to sales. It's
called How to Know a Person
>> U by David Brooks
>> and he talks about there's two types of
people. There's illuminators and there's
diminishers.
And illuminators um shine the light on
the individual. They're curious. They're
open. They're getting to know the
person. It's about them. And in sales,
this is this is a a really good
attribute to help illuminate their
customers or a sales leader to
illuminate the candidates, to help shine
in them what's already there.
>> Diminishers make it about them.
>> Yeah.
>> They tie it back to their own stories.
They're toppers. They hear a story and
then they'll top with their own story.
when I was a rep. And the reality is is
reps rarely care what their sales
manager did as a performer.
>> And so you can hang on to it, but that's
actually diminishing
>> the the other person because you're
making it about you. So really good
sales leaders
>> forget about what they did as individual
contributors and are solely focused on
illuminating the talents and helping the
other people become the best they can
be. the greatest one of the greatest
um functions of high performers and
people performing at their highest is
their level of confidence in the moment.
>> And so if we know that it's about them
being confident in the moment when it's
time to perform, then we become
confidence givers. And in the field,
what this looks like is it's not giving
them like coaching or feedback while
you're standing in the lobby. And I see
this all the times. Um, I've written
with a lot of sales leaders and I
observe their experience as they're
coaching the reps and it'll be on the
way to, okay, don't forget this,
remember this, hey, what are we gonna do
here? And they're and they're yelling
from the sidelines during the game
fundamentals they need to follow.
>> The challenge becomes that's not that's
not confidence building.
>> You know, confidence could be building
is like you got this.
>> Yeah.
>> Right. Do what you do,
>> right? You practice for this moment. You
be you, right? you. And so that's that's
the kind of language and verbiage that's
more helpful in walking to a into a
prospect or a call is that we prepared
for this, right?
>> No need to be nervous because you've
done this 100 times. You got this. Uh
and so it's it's really important to
develop confidence in our people.
>> Yeah. I think it's one thing that's um I
see so many leaders who don't develop
confidence in their people and I think I
see a lot of times they say oh yeah the
way we do is um when they get when they
when they get hired we give a bunch of
product knowledge training.
>> Yeah.
>> So their confidence comes from product
knowledge and not not anything else. So
as a leader let's say you're a frontline
sales dude. How do you help instill that
confidence into your team?
>> Yeah it's a great question. I'll give an
example. Um, I was out uh with a new rep
and we were doing calls and we went into
a lobby and it was one of those where
there's a keypad where you have to type
in somebody's name in order to get
through.
>> So, we're in the lobby, there's a locked
door and there's a keypad. And he said
to me, "I don't know what to do. What
what what what do I do now? As a leader,
I'm my goal is to build confidence." And
I said, "If I wasn't here, what would
you do?"
>> And he sat there, I don't know. I said,
"Well, just take a shot at it." Well,
why don't you just tell me? I said,
'Well, my my job as a coach and as a
leader is to help develop you, not not
to tell you what to do all the time.
Okay. He said, 'I think I think I would
actually knock on the door. And I said,
great, let's knock on the door.
>> And so, you may you may think, well,
punch in a name, you know, go out, look
on LinkedIn, figure out who the contact
is so you can ask for them. But I was
good with what this rep was going to do.
So, he knocks on the door. Just so
happens that this time somebody answers
the door and we have a meeting. It
worked out. And so having them come up
with their own. So now that rep's not
going to call me next time.
>> He's not going to call me and say, "Hey,
what should I do?" Because he has the
confidence that I believe he's going to
be able to do the thing.
>> What pricing should I go in at? Well,
what do you what do you think we should
go in at? Well, I think we should go in
at this percent. Okay. Well, let's do
it. Now, as a leader, I may say, "Well,
no, we need to go in at this percent or
this percent." But my job is not to fix
or control or make somebody successful.
is to develop people.
>> That's right.
>> So, I'm willing to sacrifice a little
bit of margin on that one account and
then talk about it after then telling
them what to do. So, I think that's one
way to do it
>> is to is to be a confidence giver and
look for all of those ways to build
confidence in and even when giving
feedback,
>> right? Being being a good feedback
giver.
>> So, if there was something that happened
in the call, pointing that out, having a
conversation around it and then having
them try again and encouraging. Verbal
persuasion is a way to give confidence.
>> Sure.
>> And these are all little little
techniques. So, are we good verbal
persuaders to build the confidence in
the other people?
>> Um, you know, I there was um there was a
consultant that was hired by an NBA team
to help motivate and build the
confidence in the team because they had
several losing seasons. And so the
consultant actually counted how many
fist bumps happened during the course of
a game
>> because that was little confidence boost
like oh yeah we got this and so you know
are we given fist bumps intentionally in
in offices even today
>> when I'm walking around talking to
people I prefer a fist bump over a
handshake
>> just the way that I operate. Okay
there's a little bit more like
>> teamwork involved in in a fist bump and
that's intentional
>> and so now when people see me coming
it's it's a fist bump. So, I got that
because the the consultant came in and
said, "Let's measure the fist bumps."
They actually measured and watched how
many fist bumps happened on many teams.
>> And the teams that had the best winning
record had a proportion more fist bumps
>> during free throws, during timeouts.
There were more communication, you know,
physical. I think that's indicative is
that what does that environment look
like that you're creating? Well, I think
what's what's really interesting is
like, and this is where, you know, a lot
of leaders are fast to say, "Hey, you're
doing this wrong. You're doing that
wrong." But they're not recognizing
behaviors they want to consistently see.
>> Yeah.
>> And, you know, I call it catch them
doing something right.
>> That's right.
>> So, it's like, can we catch our team
doing something right even if it's the
smallest little thing that maybe is
second nature to them, but when we can
recognize that, they're more like
continue doing that behavior. It's like,
cool. Yeah. Because I am that type. It's
a reinforcement of the identity. I am a
person who does these things. Like maybe
the call went sideways with the rep, but
they did a great job preparing. Hey, you
know what, Marcus? You did a great job
preparing. You did your research. You
did your homework. I know it didn't go
the way you wanted, but you know what?
Great job. Keep doing that.
>> Reinfor
100%.
>> I think also really interesting as well
is even your example of the rep who
wasn't sure what to do, knock on the
door, call the number, when we ask them
questions, hey, what would you if I
wasn't here, what would you do? Mhm.
>> And give them the opportunity to make a
decision is so powerful to help them
build confidence.
>> But I think on the flip side, I think
I've also seen it too and tell what you
think. The flip side, I've seen also
leaders that's all they do and they
actually don't even guide the rep. So
the rep is like, I really have no idea.
They're just there's they're so lost and
the Andrew's like, well, you should
figure it out.
>> Yeah. And I think in situations like
that, my opinion is, okay, you give them
the opportunity and they're really
struggling. They're just maybe they're
just too new, they're too scared, etc.
So, you may need to prompt them a little
bit. What's a baby step? Okay, listen.
Like, okay, here's what I want you to
try. Try this.
>> So, that and then next time it happens
again. Hey, since you already told them
what to do and they ask you the same
question again. Hey, remember last time
we came to a door, the same situation?
Yeah. What did we do then?
>> You're hitting on a really important
principle, which is situational
leadership. That's right.
>> And so as a as a sales leader, you need
to look at the situation. If this is a
brand new employee and they're asking a
question about compensation or product
knowledge,
>> we're not going to say, "Well, what do
you think?"
>> Yeah. Right.
>> So situationally, at the beginning, it's
a high level of
>> of support. It's a high level of
education
>> and it's going to be received well. And
then once you get into the tenure, they
need less of that and more. So I think
understanding when it's time to tell
them, that's
>> right.
>> When it's time to show them,
>> when it's time to watch them, right,
>> and when it's time to redirect. And the
four things are tell them, show them,
watch them, and redirect. And that's the
feedback at last. So you got to know
when to tell them.
>> You got to know when to model it and
show them. You got to know when it's
time to watch and then redirect. And
that's an easy model for sales leaders
to remember is the tell, show, watch.
>> I love that. How how would a leader know
when to pull which which one out? I
think I think a lot of leaders struggle
with this and I've seen this like I mean
I walk in so many organizations and it's
like the team literally does not know
what good looks like for so many things.
The leader is like
>> you just need to make calls. I'm telling
them what to do. You just need to work
harder. I'm telling them what to do but
it's not clear. So how do you determine
as a leader which one to pull out of
your bag?
>> This is an easy one, Marcus. Because if
you fall into the trap of saying, "I've
told them or you think I've told them."
And I hear this so much with leaders.
Let's talk about the performance. And
the sales say, "Well, I've told them or
they they know they need to do this."
Okay.
>> Well, if you've told them and it's not
getting the outcome, then it's time to
move to show them or watch them.
>> Yeah.
>> And if you show if you've shown them
over and over again and it's not getting
the result, maybe you need to go back
and do some more explanation on the
tell. So, I look at where are you at
from a result standpoint and if telling
them isn't working then you can show or
watch. I think by default sales uh
leaders are really good tellers.
>> So, if you're not sure then I would say
don't tell.
>> Right? If if if all else is equal and
you're not sure what to do or to coach
them is to avoid telling. You know,
advice often falls on a dormant brain.
If a rep isn't coming and asking
questions and we're constantly feeding
them and telling them what to do, it
becomes white noise, we don't get the
result and then we get frustration.
>> And so that's when you can catch
yourself. I've told them, I've told
them, right? You're telling maybe your
director or VP, I've told the rep this
or they understand this.
>> Well, they may know it, but they're not
able to do it. And so now it's time to
watch and redirect.
>> Oh, yeah.
>> Yeah. Yeah,
>> I mean as you're talking I was thinking
about as a rep I had years ago and uh
you know we we had a half day together
for a field ride and they have to have
there's they need a minimum three
appointments for that that field day and
uh morning doesn't have any and I'm like
hey what's what's going on? Oh, you
know, like he's new. He's like, "Oh, the
scripts don't work. You know, the list
is bad. You know, like it's not working.
What you showed me, you know, so this is
like this is like it's impossible." I
said, "Okay, that's good points. Okay,
well, let's let's sit down. Let's talk
about it." And he was complaining. I'm
like, "Cool." Like, "Well, maybe show me
how you're working the list." He's like,
"What?" I'm like, "Yeah, we'll just go
back to the office. I just I don't want
you just show me. Open the CRM up. You
open up the list and let me just see how
you work and if I can help you, I'll
help you. But if I'm completely wrong
and everything I told you doesn't make

figure it out together." He's like,
"Okay." So, we go back and I watch him
open up the CRM.
>> Yeah.
>> Immediately realize he has no idea how
to use the CRM. He's just like aimlessly
clicking around like doesn't doesn't
even know how to pull pulls countless
stuff. I'm like, "Okay, my bad."
Obviously, I did not
>> This is where we're at.
>> This is where we're at. No problem. So,
hey, try this. So, he's able to like do
it properly. And then I'm like, "Okay,
let's make some calls like right now."
I'm like,
>> "Yeah,
>> yeah,
>> go ahead. Like, I just want to see what

thing." So, you know, he dials the first

>> Have some iterations. Do more

through, finally call, goes through,
gets a little better. Fast forward two
hours, you know, ends up booking three
appointments.
>> Yeah.
>> I'm like, and and I realized at that
point I'm like, man, I thought I was
telling him what to do early. I thought
I showed him what to do, but it wasn't

leader. So after that though, I realized

really showing them what good looks like
and helping them, helping them redirect.
So that was a really good point you
brought Well, you're also modeling
accountability for that rep, too, and
taking responsibility and ownership. And
so, that's contagious as well. Oh, yeah.
>> You It also illustrates that what you
did was you actually met the rep where
they're at.
>> That's right.
>> And you help them get where they want to
go. Oh, yeah. And that's definition of
coaching to me. That'd be that'd be a
really good example of what coaching
looks like. Mhm.
>> So, I I I think that meeting them and
seeing what really is going on and it
doesn't you could have put a band-aid on
it and done field coaching, but what you
did was you went back and identified
what the what the root opportunity was
for that rep
>> that would help fix all of the other or
support or help develop all the other
things down the road. And I think that's
really really important example. Yeah, I
think at the end of the day whenever
there is a misalignment in terms of what
we expect as a leader and what they're
actually doing, if there's some sort of
constraint, we need to as leaders find
them what is the number one constraint
that's holding them back.
>> Yeah.
>> If we can solve that constraint,
obviously other constraints will show
up. If we can solve that constraint and
that might be the big domino to get
everything else going on, but if that we
don't solve a big constraint, they'll
just keep getting stuck to the point
where they basically quit you as a
leader.
>> Yeah. And we become frustrated.
>> Exactly.
>> Yeah. The the um the frustration. So
where the expectation is and where
performance is that gap I call it the
frustration gap both for the the rep or
the employee and the leader.
>> And so either the expectation is
unreasonable for that person
>> uh or there there's a they don't have
the capacity to elevate
>> to what the expectation is. But either
way there's frustration.
>> Oh yeah. And so I think as a leader we
do need to take a look at is the
expectation achievable.
>> You know we talk about confidence
giving.
>> Yeah.
>> If if we know that the expectation is
not achievable for the majority of reps
then what we're doing is we're
diminishing confidence.
>> Yeah.
>> Because they don't believe they can do
it because it has been done before. And
so if it's been done before and they can
see that it's been done or they talk to
somebody that has achieved it, then
their confidence level goes up.
>> Oh yeah.
>> Well, what's interesting is like we see
it all the time, you know, rep is given
a quot number and there's like there's I
don't even know how to get there and the
leader says good luck.
>> Yeah,
>> that's really it, right? but versus like
a really elite leader. It's like, let's
actually walk through and break down
what the math looks like so you can
actually get to that point. Like they're
breaking down like, okay, cool. You have
a million-doll number. Okay, based off
your closing ratio and your average
account size, your average deal size.
Okay, based off your activity model,
that breaks down to you need to have
five qualified meets that show up a
week. You know, you need to you'll close
X amount and you need that means you
need to make X number of dials per day.
But when they can understand the logical
math to get that point, it's realistic.
It's like,
>> yeah, you know what? I can make 20 calls
a day.
>> Yeah,
>> that's feasible.
>> Yeah.
>> You know, versus close a million dollars
and I don't know how to get there.
>> Yeah,
>> I don't see the path. Show me the Google
map path from A to Z and I'll be able to
get there.
>> It can be really overwhelming when reps
are looking at a big number. And I think
a really good leader helps them
understand what the next course of
action is going to be to help them get
the goal. you know, distill it down to
so what so what do we do next?
>> And a lot of times in like a debrief,
sitting down, I think debrief debriefs
are really important that you have a
routine. You sit down
>> and it's well I coach them in the field
or I coach them.
>> When you're in the field, we coach on
sales ability. When we're sitting down
and having a conversation as a debrief,
we're looking at results and what
actions we need to take. But at the end
of that debrief, I think it's important
to say based on everything that we've
talked about, what is the next course of
action for you?
>> What are you willing to commit to is
another really good question. And and
then writing it down. And a great way to
start the next debrief is to say, okay,
last time we spoke about this, how did
that go?
>> And that question becomes important,
too, because it isn't did you do the
thing. I say model accountability and
hold other people capable.
>> So we model the followup. we model doing
what we say we're going to do and we
believe that the other person is able to
fulfill their commitment. So that's
that's my methodology behind model
accountability. And sometimes I'll say
stop holding people accountable.
>> Model accountability. It's contagious
>> and hold other people capable. So how
did it go
>> in this activity that you committed to
do is much different than did you do the
thing. And if there's one small little
hack a sales leader can do to develop
confidence. Remember, confidence giving
>> to be a confidence giver is to is to
have positive intent with your people
>> and assume that they're going to
actually follow through with what they
did. And if they know that you believe
in them,
>> then their confidence goes up.
>> I'm always looking for ways to be a
confidence giver.
>> Well, I think and it's it's it's so
subtle but so impactful, right? Like for
example, if your action was like maybe
they're not doing a good job on
preparing for calls and that's their
only action. Hey, you have five calls
next week. I just want you to do the
research like we discussed. Look up
these things and prepare. That's all I
want you to do.
>> Yeah.
>> And then the following, hey, how did it
go with that preparation?
And their answer will tell you
everything that that you need to know,
right? Like and that is like how you
start to help instill that confidence in
them, right? And I think what's
interesting is when he's talking about
modeling accountability,
>> I'm also a firm believer your team is
always watching you.
>> Mhm.
>> Right. What are some behaviors you see
that leaders are not doing to hold
themselves accountable that their team
sees and potentially weakens their
leadership?
>> Two things. One, leaders need to make
and declare commitments to their team
>> and use that that verbiage.
>> I'm committed to
>> and then be really specific on what
you're committed to.
>> Then follow up with the team on the
commitment. So this may be this week I'm
going to get out in the field for three
days. Here's who I'm riding with. Love
it. And then next week at the sales
meeting, you talk about how those three
calls went. You talk about the success
that you had. You you illuminate the
rep. You build confidence.
>> So when I say modeling accountability,
the first thing you have to do is you
have to set a commitment. You have to
vocalize that in front of the team.
>> Behavior-based commitments,
>> the the ones that you can control
whether you achieve them or not.
>> And when you start using the word what
I'm going to commit to is or I can
commit to, right? So then you reframe as
a leader, you're always looking for ways
to make and keep commitments. That's
modeling accountability. But in order to
keep a commitment, you have to make a
commitment. And they have to know you're
making the commitment.
>> I love that.
>> Yeah.
>> Well, I think what's cool is like I was
thinking about this, right, as you were

would forget. Mhm.

like, "Yeah,
>> really?" He's like, "You guys don't just
trust me that I'm doing my job. Like,
you don't think I'm working hard?"
They're like, "Well, you know, I'm like
he's like, "You should show the team
this." I'm like, "That is actually
fascinating insight." So, at that point,
I remember every week, I would just
print out my calendar. I put it on my

repeat it to them what I was doing that
week, right? I was modeling
accountability. Now, fast forward,
remember I was in a director role and I
remember I was traveling quite often.
I'm like, this is going to be a big
issue. This is going to be the same
problem since my team all spread out.
They're they're going to have no idea.
So, I do I do the same thing. I would

team, right? So, they could see what I
was doing, right? And um is it funny? I
was housed in the Portland office and I
remember this is like I was like two

>> And he's like, "It absolutely 100% is."
And I think that's something that we

to them.
>> The ne the next sales meeting that you
have as a sales leader,
listen to if you're telling the reps, we
need to work harder, we need to do more
cold calls, we need better results.
>> You know, these are all tells. And I
always say, did they know that before?
Did they know that that they cold calls
are important? Did they know? But
modeling it, going out and just cold
calling with them is 10x the result of
saying we need to do more cold calls. In
fact, I stopped as a sales leader
talking about
>> uh
>> saying we need to work harder, right? We
really got to hit our number. Everybody
knows you need to hit your number. This
quarter is important. Everybody knows
this quarter is important. So what gets
in the way of following a good process
is the result. That's right.
>> Is worrying about the result. So as long
as you're focused on, you know, the
negative parts about what the result is,
we're going to disorient our teams into
remembering what it takes to get there.
So I learned that instead of saying we
need to do more cold calls is today
we're going to practice cold calls.
>> Instead of saying we need more time in
the field, I schedule a time to meet my
rep at 7:30 in the field. So, you're
modeling what you want the rep to do.
What you're talking about is posting a
schedule. Well, if you expect your reps
to have a call cycle or a rhythm in
their prospecting, then they see that
you have a a rhythm.
>> And so, you start as you model this as a
leader over time,
>> you find yourself not needing to have
that temptation to say, "Let's work
harder. We need to hit our number."
Everybody knows that.
>> That's not confidence giving. Confidence
giving looks like let's role play and
help improve competence so that you're
comfortable going out and doing cold
calls
>> and having fun doing it because you're
good at it.
>> Now I'm sure this happened to you Travis
because you've been really successful,
right? Like I'm sure it gets to a point
where you perform at such a high level,
right? And people like, "Wow, Travis is
a machine." Like I'm just not wired that
way. I'm not like that person. and they
don't they don't know your whole
backstory or they've forgotten, right?
How do you overcome that? How do you get
that rep or manager to
believe in what needs to be done when
they they say you're just a cut from
different cloth?
>> Yeah. Yeah. Well, I I think it's
important that in order to help help
them or or
>> maybe offer a different framework
>> is to focus on the wins that they're
getting today. Mhm.
>> And if somebody wants to make it about
me or the success, I'll often flip to,
you know, what can we do to support you?
And so it isn't necessarily like
deflecting that.
>> Um, you know, thank you and all of those
things.
>> Um, but really I'm here to support you
and it starts with
>> what's going to happen today or
tomorrow.
>> So, you know, cut from the same cloth. I
I think it's the way I would frame that
is it's more like what is what are we
capable of today
>> and how do we increase the capacity
tomorrow.
>> Yeah.
>> And so what can I do to help you get
from where you are to where you want to
go? And everybody in that where they are
to where they want to go is different.
>> And it's not good, bad, right or wrong.
>> It's just that my job as a leader is to
focus on you and helping you get to
where you're think to go and what your
goals are. what my goals are and the
luck that I've had, you know, the breaks
that have happened. Um, also the the
work that I've done to develop in terms
of just being my own best person that
was my own journey and so let's create
one for you. Let me help you create one
and doing it with really good questions
and understanding.
>> I love that. I think it's powerful
because I think it's like this ties back
to confidence, right? We're helping
install confidence in our people and
I've always seen confidence like it's
like a bank account and we we too we too
often do withdrawals versus deposits and
our job as leaders is those micro
things. It's like hey let's talk about
you and maybe you're not where you want
to be right now but what's something we
could do today? It's a a step towards
that and when they do that it's like
boom it's like a little little
confidence deposit.
>> Yeah.
>> Keep doing it over time. It compounds
more and more and hopefully that
confidence bucket overflows. Right.
Yeah. And it gets to the point where
there's like they're they believe
themselves. Yeah. They realize, hey, you
know what? Like
>> Mark's Tra,
>> you guys are nobody special.
>> Like you're we're we're not the same
person. You have flaws. We all have
flaws, but you know what? You're acting
in spite of the flaws and I can too.
>> Yeah.
>> I think that's a cool thing to
>> Absolutely.
>> Let's talk about coaching culture.
Actually, no. Let's talk about oneonone.
I'll talk about one ones. Right.
>> Right. So because I think oneon1s are a
topic where uh you mentioned already
most people do do them incorrectly and I
know you have run thousands of one-on-
ones. What happens in the first five
minutes? What's in the first five
minutes that really sets kind of sets
the tone for the rest of the half an
hour or hour?
>> Yeah. Well, often times there's
something that the rep wants to talk
about or doesn't want to talk about. And
so the first five minutes is flushing
out what's alive for them and
controlling mind share in the moment so
that we can talk about bigger picture
things.
>> Yeah.
>> So I got into the habit of asking what's
the most important thing that you want
to talk about in our time together. And
they have the first five minutes.
>> They may be frustrated in account. They
may have just walked out of a call that
didn't go well. And so we need to
regulate and and flush that out and then
get into the the routine of the debrief
and and follow up and setting
commitments and and looking at numbers
and whatever that structure is going to
be.
>> But in the first five minutes, it's
really important for me to get to a spot
where that conversation can matter. And
so it's about flushing out what's alive
for them and kind of getting back into
regulating and then saying, "Okay, after
that's flushed out, if it's a bigger
topic too, Marcus, I I think you can
say, "Okay, well, let's table that.
Let's meet next Friday morning for
coffee and we'll talk about that
situation."
>> So, let's put that aside for now.
>> We're going to take care of it in the
appropriate time. And then I go to last
time, here are the commitments, you
know, and so how did this go? How how
did that call go? How did the
conversation go with with your client
that you were going to have?
>> And so rolling once we get out of the
what's top of mind, what's alive for
them right now, what's taking up their
mind share. Once that's flushed away for
a few minutes, then we can get into
following up on their commitments and
then we can talk about their numbers,
whatever else we want to talk about for
performance and then set more more
action items. So to me it became, you
know, eliminating the tension, whatever
is alive,
>> following up on commitments,
setting new commitments. So the debrief
and I always said 25 minutes
>> and stick to the 25 minutes. If they're
carrying over, we're we're not being
efficient just like in our time and
scheduling. So, I think it's important
to condense those those weekly or every
other week debriefs to a certain amount
of time. I like 25 minutes because it
wasn't quite a half an hour. We could
stick to it. Y
>> uh but it's it's very routine and it's
very structured so they know what to
expect.
>> Nobody likes to be surprised when they
sit down with their manager.
>> And so that running agenda and if
there's something outside of that you
need to address, just address it outside
of the debrief,
>> right? Don't don't project your own need
to have resolution on an item onto them.
There's topics to talk at a sales
meeting about. There's topics and
coaching to talk in the field and
there's topics to talk about in a
debrief. And I always say don't blend
those together.
>> Sales meeting topic.
>> It is for everybody.
>> Coaching in the field, sales capacity,
debriefs were talking about numbers and
setting action items. So if ever I was
tempted because something came up, they
missed their number, now you're in the
field with them. I'm not talking about
them missing their number while we're
coaching in the field. That's going to
be during the debrief, right?
>> So we're hyperfocused on developing in
the moment on where we're at.
>> I love that. I think it's like mistake I
see a lot of times is the one ones are
not structured at all.
>> Uh or they allow the rep to hijack the
whole conversation about whatever stuff.
>> At the end of the day, that oneonone's
designed for really two things. Number
one, they know exactly where they stand.
and what to do next
>> and they can walk away with that exact
plan. It's like cool, we're good because
we have these other blocks and times,
right, for coaching and and the team as
well, but having those things dialed and
you you absolutely nailed that, right?
So, I go back to hiring. We know
one bad hire is very expensive. So, what
what do you think the real number is of
one bad hire on a 10erson team?
>> Well, one, I think every company needs
to actually come up with that number. If
you don't know the number and you're not
able to talk about that number, then
it's not probably going to motivate you
into changing some things about your
hiring practices. So, the first thing I
would say is that know what that number
is. It's well into six figures. Oh,
really?
>> I think it's not just the salary that
you lose. So, if it's a $60,000 base
salary sales rep
>> and they last for a year and you know,
and then you cut them after a year, it's
not the $60,000 salary. uh it's what the
opportunity cost was for all of the
people that contributed including your
own time as a sales manager. So I would
have sales managers go back and
calculate how many hours a week they
were talking to or training. Then I
would take the sales manager salary and
divide it out and say, "Okay, so this
was X amount that we spent
>> for you to coach this rep.
>> It was X amount on their salary. We're
going to add those up." And then I look
at every other touch point that was
taking time to help the rep. Oh yeah,
>> right. The training. So maybe it was
people that that came into the field
with them. Maybe it was customer
service. Maybe it was, you know, all of
these people that helped support the
rep. You have to add up all of those.
Then you have to look at what all of
those people could have been doing.
>> Oh yeah.
>> So it's it's not just the cost of the
dollar amount. It's also the opportunity
cost of all of the other functions of
those individuals, all those other
>> uh areas that they helped with. And then
you add that cost into it. $100,000 is
probably really low when you factor
that.
>> Um, but I think it can be sticker shock
even that number. Uh, and so but the
more pain that you feel around reality
can also just be a little bit of a
wakeup call. If you don't know that
exact number and you haven't done the
calculation and and and looked at all of
the different components, then you
probably won't change much about your
hiring practice and you'll chalk it up
to, well, the candidate was lazy. Well,
I think it's like it's interesting is
like you I didn't even think about the
other people that are probably helping
as part of the process, right? When you
think let's say it's 100k base salary
just for simple math plus the time of
the manager, time of people on your
team. Let's just call it like $200,000
minimum cost already.
>> But then I think about like let's say
it's a million dollar a year territory
and they put call 200 grand.
>> Yep.
>> That's like $800 grand opportunity loss.
Yep. Absolutely.
>> But think about even the people they
talk to they probably botched calls on.
So now they did a terrible job with
prospects, reputational equity and all
these things. So now your costs are
skyrocketing.
>> I think that's why it's like
>> hiring great people, keeping them in the
seat is like the number one priority
that we can do as a great leader.
>> So be be uh be really good at
interviewing and always be recruiting.
>> Amazing conversation, Travis. People
want to learn more about you, get a hold
of you. Where can they find?
>> I would say growthwithtravis.com. Just
come to my website. There's a contact me
page. Um, some some content on there
that I think uh would be interesting and
provocative. So, yeah,
growthwithtravis.com.
>> Thanks for being on.
>> Thank you.
>> That's a wrap on this episode of the
revenue vault. Now, if one line stuck
with you, let it be this. The best
salespeople don't fix people. They build
the environment, ask the harder
questions, and let the rep prove what
they're already capable of. Now, Travis
has been running that playbook for 25
